# Retention Insights Strategy

## Purpose

Define the future reporting and retention direction for Chore Wars so the app stays useful after the novelty wears off.

## Why retention matters

Points and one-tap completion make the app fast. They do not, by themselves, explain the household story over time. Families, couples, flatmates, students, and shared homes are more likely to keep using Chore Wars when it shows visible fairness, habit patterns, and a lightweight reason to return each week.

## Why points alone are not enough

- points show activity, but not the pattern behind it
- points reward completion, but do not explain fairness
- points can make a single week exciting, but not necessarily habit-forming
- without insight, the app risks feeling like a score counter instead of a household habit tool

## What makes Chore Wars unique

- it combines fairness, competition, and humour
- it is built for shared homes rather than solo productivity
- it can show who is carrying the household without becoming shaming
- it can make invisible chores visible in a playful way
- it can turn weekly household life into something people want to check again

## Target retention audiences

- families
- couples
- flatmates or shared houses
- students
- young professionals

## Fairness insights strategy

Show whether the household feels balanced, not just who won.

Possible insights:

- who did the most this week
- who improved most
- who is carrying the household
- who did the invisible chores
- member contribution percentage
- chore balance or fairness score
- weekly winner
- bottom-place trend
- one chore could change the leaderboard

Fairness insights should be light, understandable, and emotionally safe. They should help households talk about chores instead of arguing about them.

## Household habit trends strategy

Surface what the household actually does over time:

- most completed chore
- most ignored chore
- busiest chore day
- weekend versus weekday patterns
- Sunday household reset day
- chores that tend to be forgotten
- chores that keep bouncing back
- household habit shifts over time

These trends should help households notice routines, not lecture them.

## Weekly summary strategy

Give households a short weekly recap that feels worth checking.

Possible summary elements:

- weekly winner
- bottom-place member
- most completed chore
- most improved member
- busiest day
- balance or fairness snapshot
- a playful line that makes the summary feel fun

Example copy:

- “The bins were defeated twice this week.”
- “Bathroom duty remains the final boss.”
- “Sunday is your household reset day.”
- “One chore could change the leaderboard.”

## Personal stats strategy

Give each member a small personal history without turning the app into a solo fitness tracker.

Possible personal stats:

- personal best week
- chores completed this week
- contribution percentage
- favourite chore
- most improved week
- top weekend effort
- streaks, if added later

Personal stats should feel motivating rather than competitive in a harsh way.

## Playful copy and personality strategy

The voice should be:

- friendly
- funny
- lightly competitive
- never cruel
- never childish
- never overly corporate

Example lines:

- “Mandy carried 42% of this week’s chores.”
- “Alex is only 3 points from the top.”
- “The dog has accepted your service.”
- “The bathroom is still the final boss.”
- “The leaderboard never lies.”

## Rewards and forfeits retention strategy

Rewards and forfeits should make insights feel meaningful:

- weekly summaries can point to the house rule
- winner and bottom-place data can feed playful reward/forfeit cards
- insights should reinforce fairness, not shame
- reward and forfeit copy should stay short and household-configurable

## Future email and push notification strategy

Later, Chore Wars can use summaries to bring people back:

- weekly email summary
- weekly fairness recap
- reminder that the household week has changed
- push notification for weekly winner or summary

These should come after the in-app insights prove useful.

## Data privacy considerations

- keep insight data household-scoped
- avoid exposing unnecessary personal details
- use friendly summaries rather than sensitive breakdowns
- do not over-collect data just to make charts look busy
- keep future retention messaging aligned with what the app actually stores

## What not to build yet

- heavy dashboards
- spreadsheet-like reports
- endless charts for every metric
- complex streak systems before the basic household story is clear
- invasive notifications before in-app summaries are useful
- email automation before the in-app reporting loop is proven

## Suggested future phases

- insights and fairness reports
- weekly household summaries
- retention loops inside the app
- later Resend email summaries
- later push notifications
- later streaks or long-term habit milestones if they add real value

## Acceptance criteria for a useful insights feature

- households can understand who is doing the work
- the app highlights fairness, not just points
- weekly summaries feel worth checking
- the tone stays playful and human
- the insights help people come back without making the app feel like a boring task manager
- the app can support repeat weekly use for families, couples, flatmates, students, and young professionals
